---
title: "Concept Activation Vectors"
author: Kris Sankaran
date: 2026-04-13
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    fig-align: center
    code-overflow: wrap
execute:
    echo: true
    message: false
    warning: false
    cache: false
    eval: true
---

::: {style="display: none;"}
$$
\newcommand{\bs}[1]{\mathbf{#1}}
\newcommand{\reals}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\widebar}[1]{\overline{#1}}
\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
\newcommand{\indic}[1]{\mathbb{1}\left\{{#1}\right\}}
\newcommand{\Earg}[1]{\mathbb{E}\left[{#1}\right]}
\newcommand{\Esubarg}[2]{\mathbb{E}_{#1}\left[{#2}\right]}
$$
:::

<style>
.purple { color: #7458d1ff; } /* pastel purple */
.orange { color: #fca020; } /* pastel orange */
.green { color: #3bbe67ff; } /* pastel green */
.darkblue { color: #4a9ceaff; } /* pastel dark blue */
.pink { color: #ee6ec3ff; } /* pastel pink */
</style>

```{r}
#| label: fig-setup
#| echo: false
library(tidyverse)
library(reticulate)
theme_set(theme_classic() + theme(panel.border= element_rect(fill = NA, linewidth = .5)))
set.seed(2026)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-pysetup
#| echo: false
# includes the SHAP package. Can install it using,
# > conda env create -f stat479_week13.yaml
# where the yaml file is located at: https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/notes/stat479_week13.yaml
use_condaenv("stat479_week13_demo")
```

_[Readings]()_, _[Code](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/notes/14-concepts_handout.qmd)_,  _[Conda Env](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/notes/stat479_week14.yaml)_

We added background on TCAV that could appear in our exams. Items marked
$^{\dagger}$ will not be tested.

## Setup

1. **Goal**. Determine whether a deep learning model $f$ uses an abstract
concept when predicting a particular class. Further, localize the use of that
concept to particular layers within the model.

1. **Requirement**. We want a quantitative measure of concept importance in a
model's prediction. An approach that leaves too much to subjective
interpretation will not be sufficient.

    - Running example: Modern vision models trained on imagenet can accurately
    classify zebra images. Do they use the concept "stripes"?

    - We could apply saliency maps to see whether the classification is
    sensitive to the parts of the image with the zebra, but this doesn't tell us
    whether it's actually using the abstract "stripes" concept.

      ::: {#fig-stripes-examples}
      ::: {.columns}
      ::: {.column width="37%"}
      ![](figures/Common_zebra_1.jpg){width=100%}
      :::
      ::: {.column width="6%"}

      :::
      ::: {.column width="30%"}
      ![](figures/green-vibrant-tropical-leaves-with-detailed-stripes-pattern.jpg){width=100%}
      :::
      :::
      We recognize the stripes concept in both images, but does our model?
      :::

1. **Approach.** Require the user to specify the abstract concept through a
curated set of examples. Given these examples, learn a _concept activation
vector_ (CAV) representing the concept in a user-specified layer's activation
space and derive a test to see whether the direction is relevant for
classification into the class of interest.

## Motivation

1. _Quantitative Concept Importance_. We motivated deep learning by
hypothesizing that deep layers captured more complex, meaningful concepts. Our
own examples suggested gave some evidence for this (e.g., the emotion probing
experiment). Nonetheless, we haven't seen any way to turn this heuristic into a
precise, quantitative method for evaluating or finding concepts in our own data
and models.

   - Bias: When a model classifies an image as "Programmer," did it use the
   "Gender" concept? We want a quantitative measure to support formal auditing.

1. _Shared language_. A more philosophical motivation is that finding
representations that are shared by both people and machine learning models would
make it easier to describe, control, and learn from the models. From this
perspective, we need ways to put human and models representations in direct
dialog with one another.

    ![From Been Kim's 2024 ICLR keynote.](figures/kim_iclr.png){width=80%}

1. This direct interface is especially important for tasks that require
augmentation (tools that help people solve problems more effectively) rather
than automation (taking the human out of the loop). For example, the GAN
dissection program learned concepts for different parts of landscapes and
allowed photoshop-like manipulation using representations of concepts like
"grass" and "dome,"

    ![This [website](https://gandissect.csail.mit.edu/) has other interactive examples.](figures/network_dissection.gif){width=60%}

## Concept Vectors

1. _Notation_. We divide the model $f$ at layer $l$, writing it as $f = h_{l}
\circ g_{l}$. The function $g_l$ maps the input $x$ to layer $l$'s activations,
while $h_{l}$ transforms those intermediate activations to the model's final
predictions.

   ![The original model $f$.](figures/concepts_overall_model.png){width=60%}

   ![The model $f$ split at layer $l$.](figures/concepts_split_model.png){width=60%}

   The class of interest is $k$. We also require sets of concept-relevant and
   random reference images^[For concreteness, we'll discuss image concepts, but
   the method applies to other modalities as well.], denoted $\mathcal{D}_{c}$
   and $\mathcal{D}_{r}$, respectively. We also set $\mathcal{D} =
   \mathcal{D}_{c} \cup \mathcal{D}_{r}$ and $N_{c}, N_{r}, N$ to be the sizes
   of $\mathcal{D}_{c}$, $\mathcal{D}_{r}$, and $\mathcal{D}$, respectively.

   - In the zebra example, $k$ is the class "zebra", $c$ is the concept
   "stripes", and $\mathcal{D}_{c}$ would be images with stripes more generally (e.g.,
    tigers, flags, candy canes, ...)

   ![We provide examples $\mathcal{D}_{c}$ to encapsulate what we mean by a concept.](figures/concepts_step1.png){width=70%}

1. Geometrically, if a model layer has "learned" concept $c$, then we expect
images representing that concept to all be relatively more close together than
the random references images.  Conversely, if the model is "unaware" of this
concept, then it should be scattered in a way similar to the random images.

   ![If a concept is learned by a layer, then it is concentrated in a particular region of its activation space.](figures/concepts_model_learned.png){width=75%}

   _Exercise: In the motivating example, the colors below correspond to which option below?_
      - _Orange $\to$ random images, Blue $\to$ zebra images_
      - _Orange $\to$ stripes images, Blue $\to$ zebra images_
      - _Orange $\to$ zebra images, Blue $\to$ stripes images_
      - _Orange $\to$ random images, Blue $\to$ stripes images_

1. To make this intuition precise, write the average layer-$l$ activation across
samples in $\mathcal{D}$ and $\mathcal{D}_{c}$ as,
   \begin{align}
   \hat{\mu}_{\mathcal{D}} := \frac{1}{N} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{D}} g_{l}\left(x\right) \qquad \hat{\mu}_{c} := \frac{1}{N_{c}}\sum_{x \in \mathcal{D}_{c}} g_{l}\left(x\right)
   \end{align}
   and define the _concept activation vector_ (CAV) $v_{c}^{l}$ as,
   $$
    v_{c}^{l} \propto \hat{\mu}_{c} - \hat{\mu}_{\mathcal{D}}
   $$ {#eq-cav}
   with length normalized so that $\|v_{c}^{l}\|_{2} = 1$.

   ![](figures/mu_definitions_concepts.png)

   _Exercise: Write pseudocode for learning the FastCAV concept vector $v_{c}^{l}$ Clearly specify the pseudocode inputs._

1. The original approach to CAV @pmlr-v80-kim18d defined $v_{c}^{l}$
using support vector machines (SVM) trained to distinguish between
$\mathcal{D}_{c}$ and $\mathcal{D}_{r}$. This could be computationally
expensive, and @niebling2025 argue that the much faster difference-in-means
approach above gives comparable results as the original SVM approach.

1. Even though this approach bypasses SVM training, it is actually quite related
to a different classification algorithm, Linear Discriminant Analysis.
Specifically, suppose that the activations had been drawn from a mixture of two
Gaussians,
   \begin{align}
   g_{l}\left(x_{i}\right) \sim \begin{cases}
   \mathcal{N}\left(\mu_c, \Sigma\right) \text{ if } x \in \mathcal{D}_{c} \\
   \mathcal{N}\left(\mu_{r}, \Sigma\right) \text{ if } x \in \mathcal{D}_{r}
   \end{cases}.
   \end{align}
  Then, the vector $\Sigma^{-1}\left(\mu_{c} - \mu_{r}\right)$ is called the
  discriminant vector, and it is orthogonal to the classification decision
  boundary. The relative sizes $N_c$ and $N_r$ translate the decision boundary
  but do not change its orientation.

   ![The inverse covariance $\Sigma^{-1}$ tilts the decision boundary to better separate the classes.](figures/concepts_lda_intuition.png){width=70%}

1. In the case that $\Sigma = I$, the discriminant vector is the same as the CAV
in @eq-cav. In principle, we could estimate $\Sigma$ that is different from $I$,
and we could even allow it to differ across $\mathcal{D}_{c}$ and $\mathcal{D}_{r}$. Estimating a
covariance in higher-dimensions can be challenging, however, and isn't
considered in the reading.

1. The directions $v_{c}^{l}$ can be used to test whether concept $c$'s
layer-$l$ representation is used when predicting class $k$. First, define the
sensitivity of class $k$ to changes in layer $l$'s activations,
   \begin{align}
   s_{k,l}(x) &= \nabla h_{l,k}\left(g_{l}\left(x\right)\right)
   \end{align}
    where $h_{l,k}$ is the $k^{th}$ coordinate of $h_{l}$. The $j^{th}$
    coordinate of $s_{k,l}\left(x\right)$ tells us how a perturbation in
    the $j^{th}$ dimension of sample $x$'s layer-$l$ activation
    increases/decreases the probability of class $k$.

   ![Perturbing sample $x_n$ in the direction $s_{k,l}\left(x_n\right)$ increases $x_n$'s predicted probability for class $k$.](figures/score_definition.png){width=65%}

   _Exercise: In the motivating example, the green subset corresponds to which of the below?_
      - _Images from the zebra class._
      - _Random reference images $\mathcal{D}_{r}$._
      - _Images representing the concept $\mathcal{D}_{c}$._
      - _The union of the reference and concept images $\mathcal{D}$._

1. Next we can define the score,
   \begin{align}
   T_{c,k} = \frac{\left| \left\{ x_n \in \text{Class } k : s_{k,l}(x_n)^\top v_c^l > 0 \right\} \right|}{\left| \left\{ x_n \in \text{Class } k \right\} \right|}
   \end{align}
   which is the proportion of examples where the class $k$ sensitivity is
   aligned with the concept vector. If this is large, then we believe that the
   model is indeed using class $c$ in making its class $k$ classification.

   ![If many of class $k$'s sensitivities point into the same half-space as the concept vector, then that is evidence that the concept is used to predict class $k$.](figures/score_vs_concept_alignment.png){width=65%}

   _Exercise: If the angle between $s_{k,l}\left(x_n\right)$ and $v_{c}^{l}$ is 110 degrees, does this specific image contribute a 1 or a 0 to the numerator of the TCAV score_

1. We can test the significance of the association between $c$ and $k$ by
estimating concepts $\tilde{v}_{c,b}^{l}$ based on $B$ randomly chosen concept
sets $\left(\widetilde{\mathcal{D}}_{c}^{b}\right)_{b = 1}^{B}$.

   ![As a negative control, we define concept vectors using random control subsets.](figures/random_baselines.png){width=65%}

    The scores $\tilde{T}_{c,k}^{b}$ derived from these negative control
    concepts define a null distribution that can be used to compute a $p$-value
    for the observed $T_{c,k}$,
   \begin{align}
    p := \frac{1}{B}\left|\left\{b : \tilde{T}_{c,k}^{b} > T_{c,k}\right\}\right|
   \end{align}
   Since we need to compute $B$ CAVs, the speedup from using @eq-cav rather than
   an SVM adds up to a big difference in practice.

   ![The test statistics derived from the control concepts can be used to define a $p$-value.](figures/concepts_null_distribution.png){width=40%}

1. The TCAV test is summarized below. Given the concept vector $v_{c}^{l}$, we
can compute scores $T_{c,k}$ using `T_score`,
   ```
   T_score(model M, class k, images X_k, CAV v):
     (h_l, g_l) = M # split model into post/pre layer l
     aligned = 0
     for x in X_k: # images labeled as class k
       s = ∇_a h_lk(a) at a = g_l(x) # class k sensitivity in activation space
       if dot(s, v) > 0:
         aligned += 1
     return aligned / |X_k|
   ```
    Given this function, we can compute a reference distribution with random
    concepts to get a $p$-value.
   ```
   TCAV(M, k, X_k, D_c, D_r, B):
     # Step 1: TCAV score on real concept
     v = FastCAV(M, D_c, D_r)
     T_observed = T_score(M, k, X_k, v)

     # Step 2: null distribution using random concepts
     D = D_c ∪ D_r
     T_null = []
     for b in 1 ... B:
       D_rand = random_subset(D)
       v_rand = FastCAV(M, D_rand, D_r)
       T_null[b] = T_score(M, k, X_k, v_rand)

     p = |{b : T_null[b] >= T_observed}| / B
     return T_observed, p
   ```

   _Exercise: @Ramaswamy2023 ran an experiment to see how the choice of $D_{c}$
   can impact the final results. Specifically, they used two datasets (Ade20K
   and Pascal) that both gave sets of images representing ceiling, box, etc. The AUCs below give the classification accuracy for distinguishing $D_{c}$ vs. $D_{r}$ using the two choices of concept datasets. The cosine similarities measure the similarity of the learned $v_{c}^{l}$. Comment on the implications of this result._

   ![TCAV experiment from @Ramaswamy2023.](figures/probe_dataset_dependence.png)


## Extensions

1. $^\dagger$ _Multiple Concepts_. The proposal above doesn't allow us to work
with multiple concepts $v_{1}^{l}, \dots, v_{C}^{l}$ simultaneously. For this,
@yeh2020 propose an extension. Instead of considering alignment to individual
concept directions, we proceed in two steps.  First, we learn the relationship
between the layer $l$ activations and the class $k$ predictions,
   \begin{align}
    \hat{w}, \hat{b} := \arg\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i = 1}^{n} L\left(f_{k}\left(x_n\right), w^\top g_{l}\left(x_n\right) + b\right).
   \end{align}
  where $f_{k}$ is the model class $k$ prediction probabiliyt and $L$ is an
  appropriate loss function. This is essentially training a probe on using layer
  $l$'s activations, like in the emotion recognition demo.

1. $^\dagger$ In step two we run another regression to see how the probe's
coefficients can be expanded in terms of known concepts.
   \begin{align}
    \hat{\alpha} = \arg\min_{\alpha \succ 0} \|w - \left[v_{1}^{l} \dots v_{C}^{l}\right]\|_{2}^{2} + \lambda \|\alpha\|_{1}
   \end{align}
    The constraint $\alpha \succ 0$ ensures that the weights are nonnegative, so
    we don't have to worry about concepts cancelling one another out. The
    penalty $\lambda \|\alpha\|_{1}$ is similar to the lasso and encourages a
    representation of the probe using only a few concepts.

1. $^\dagger$ A large coefficient $\hat{\alpha}_c$ means that concept
$v_{c}^{l}$ is important for this layer's contribution to the class $k$
prediction.  Further, if our approximation error is large, then the model must
be relying on information that's not captured in the concepts that we curated in
advance -- we can quantify the extent to which we can't explain the model's
reasoning.

## Code Example

1. Let's see how concept activation vectors can be used to explain the Google
LeNet image classification model. Specifically, we'll follow the captum package
tutorial to see whether the model uses the concept of "stripes" to classifying
images into the "zebra" class. To define the concepts, we download reference
images from the Broden catalog. Both the concepts and the images to classify are
stored in [this zip
file](https://drive.google.com/file/d/18dDYwSH-OiovV8vDfmu8eMKIVFuweOy9/view?usp=sharing),
which can be unzipped using
   ```
tar -zxvf data.tar.gz
   ```
   Make sure the results are stored in a directory data/concepts/ relative to
   this notebook (or be prepared to change the paths below). The block below
   loads libraries which are already installed in the conda environment linked
   above.

   ```{python}
   #| label: fig-imports
from pathlib import Path
from torchvision import transforms
import PIL
import captum as cp
import captum.concept._utils.data_iterator as di
import glob
import torch
import torchvision
   ```

1. We'll need the helper functions imported below. These are doing some routine
image loading and transformation steps and are not specific to concept models in
particular.

   ```{python}
#| label: load-helpers
import tcav_helpers as helpers
   ```

1. `captum` defines a `Concept` class. This is basically a glorified data
loader, only really different from ordinary data loaders because it is
associated with a human interpretable label, `name`. The concept objects created
by the factory below will loop over the images within the `concept_path/name`
subdirectory.
   ```{python}
   #| label: assemble-helper
from pathlib import Path
import captum.concept._utils.data_iterator as di

def assemble_concept(name, id, concept_path):
    """Create a Concept object for captum TCAV analysis.

    Parameters
    ----------
    name : str
        Name of the concept (used as subdirectory name)
    id : int
        Unique identifier for the concept
    concept_path : Path
        Path to the directory containing concept subdirectories

    Returns
    -------
    cp.concept.Concept
        A captum Concept object with the specified name and data
    """
    dataset = di.CustomIterableDataset(helpers.load_tensor, f"{str(concept_path / name)}/")
    concept_iter = di.dataset_to_dataloader(dataset)
    return cp.concept.Concept(id=id, name=name, data_iter=concept_iter)
   ```

1. We now define 5 different instances of this Concept object. We are mainly
interested in the `striped` concept, because this seems like it should be
related to the zebra classification. All the other concepts are a form of
control.  `dotted` is a control where there is some systematic structure in the
concept, but we don't think it should be related to zebra classification at all.
The other three are random subsets of imagenet which don't share any systematic
structure.  These are a somewhat more distant control.

   ```{python}
   #| label: fig-concepts
concept_path = Path("data/concepts/")
stripes_concept = assemble_concept("striped", 0, concept_path=concept_path)
dotted_concept = assemble_concept("dotted", 1, concept_path=concept_path)
random0_concept = assemble_concept("random500_0", 2, concept_path=concept_path)
random1_concept = assemble_concept("random500_1", 3, concept_path=concept_path)
random2_concept = assemble_concept("random500_2", 4, concept_path=concept_path)
   ```

1. We will explain the Google LeNet model loaded in the block below. We need to
access activations, but we won't be doing any training, so we call
`model.eval()`.

   ```{python}
   #| label: fig-model
model = torchvision.models.googlenet(pretrained=True)
model = model.eval()
   ```

1. The TCAV implementation in captum has an object-oriented design. We first
define a generic TCAV explainer associated with a model and set of layers of
interest (in this cass, a few of the `inception4` layers). We can then apply
this object to arbitrary concept objects and response classes. Since the object
is already associated with a model/layers, we no longer need to specify the
model or layers in each call -- we can just ask whether a concept is related to
a class, and the TCAV explainer object will know to look it up in the correct
model. Notice that we are using integrated gradients to define the class'
sensitivity to perturbations to the model activations. This is different from
the original TCAV paper, which just ordinary gradients.

   ```{python}
#| label: fig-tcav

import captum as cp
layers=['inception4c', 'inception4d', 'inception4e']

tcav = cp.concept.TCAV(
    model=model,
    layers=layers,
    layer_attr_method=cp.attr.LayerIntegratedGradients(model, None, multiply_by_inputs=False)
)
   ```

1. Finally, we can apply this object to see whether stripes are related to zebra
classification. Our first version defines the concept direction by classifying
stripes images vs. random imagenet images. Here, 340 is the label for the zebra
class in imagenet. `n_steps` refers to the number of steps in the integrated
gradients approximation.

   ```{python}
   #| label: fig-stripes-random
import torch

classification_data = [[stripes_concept, random0_concept]]
zebra_images = helpers.load_tensors('zebra', transform_flag=False)
zebra_tensors = torch.stack([helpers.transform(img) for img in zebra_images])

response_id = 340
tcav_scores = tcav.interpret(
    inputs=zebra_tensors[:4],
    experimental_sets=classification_data[:4],
    n_steps=5,
    target=response_id
)
   ```

1. The `sign_count` output below is the test statistic introduced in our notes.
Values close to one mean that the class gradients are almost always in the same
half space as the concept activation direction . We see that the striped class
is generally close to 1.

   ```{python}
   #| label: fig-scores-random
   #| echo: false
import pprint
print(pprint.pformat(tcav_scores))
   ```

1. The blocks below repeat this analysis with the dotted rather than random
control. Again, we see that the striped class defines as concept activation
direction that is much more relevant to zebra class prediction.

   ```{python}
   #| label: fig-stripes-dotted
classification_data = [[stripes_concept, dotted_concept]]

tcav_scores = tcav.interpret(
    inputs=zebra_tensors[:4],
    experimental_sets=classification_data[:4],
    n_steps=5,
    target=response_id
)
   ```
   ```{python}
   #| label: tcav-print
   #| echo: false
print(pprint.pformat(tcav_scores))
   ```
